summary: Fowler examines the relationship between language workbenches and Model Driven Architecture (MDA), finding that MDA standards are largely unsuitable for language workbench development. He identifies three incompatible camps within MDA: UML PIM, MOF, and Executable UML, with only the first two relevant to language-oriented programming. While MOF shows some promise as an interchange mechanism for DSL schemas, both MDA camps fail to address the critical components of editors and generators. Fowler concludes that MDA standards were designed for different purposes and that premature standardization could harm the still-immature language workbench field.
key_points: MDA encompasses three 'mutually disdainful camps' with incompatible visions: UML PIM, MOF, and Executable UML, creating confusion about what MDA actually means
UML is too complex and semantically incoherent to serve as a foundation for language workbenches - it's 'like building a speedboat out of a truck because they both have a steering wheel'
MOF is a better fit than UML for DSL schemas but still lacks support for editors and generators, two of the three essential language workbench components
Microsoft, Intentional Software, and MPS all ignore MDA standards not out of malice but because these standards don't fit their needs
Model Driven Development practitioners often underestimate the importance of generators, treating code generation as a 'trivial implementation issue' when it actually defines DSL semantics
money_quotes: Steve Cook described the variation as falling into three mutually disdainful camps
UML proponents proud to say that UML has no semantics
It's rather like saying we should build a speedboat out of a truck because they both have a steering wheel
In my view you define standards once you've figured out common elements of working technologies. Language workbenches are too immature to be ready for standards yet
The tendency to play down generators is a major reason why so many programmers don't take modelers seriously
I think the UML is too complex and is too semantically incoherent to act as a serious base for future work
First you need to figure out how to do something, then you should figure out what and how to standardize
key_insight: Standards should follow working technology, not precede it - MDA's premature standardization makes it unsuitable for language workbenches, which need to mature before standardization can be meaningful.
